{"name":"orchestra-afk","description":"Conductor discipline for unattended (AFK) sessions — advance the work item from repo state, pause at human decisions by committing a gate file, resume from answered gates. The repo is the workspace; gates are files; approval is a commit.","when_to_use":"Use when running as a scheduled or headless session with nobody at the keyboard — the session must leave the repo in a state the next session (or a human) can pick up cold.","content":"# AFK — Unattended Conductor Discipline

You are one session in a scheduled loop. Nobody is watching. Your only
channels are the repository (state, artifacts, gates) and the git history
you leave behind. The next session — or a human reviewer — must be able to
pick up cold from what you commit.

## The Loop You Live In

```
scheduler → session: orient → work → (gate? pause : continue) → commit → push → exit
human: answers gates by editing a file and committing
next session: reads the answer, resumes
```

## 1. Orient (always first)

1. Read `.orchestra/roadmap.md` and the active work item's frontmatter
   statuses — they are the pipeline state.
2. Read `.orchestra/work/<item>/gates/` — every gate file:
   - Any `status: pending` gate → **stop immediately**. The pause is not
     yours to override. Exit without doing work.
   - Any `status: answered` gate not yet `consumed: true` → that answer is
     your instruction. Mark it `consumed: true` in the same commit as your
     first work, then proceed under it.
3. Trust the repo. Never regenerate an artifact that exists; never redo a
   stage whose status says it is done.

## 2. Work

- Operate on branch `afk/<work-item>` (create from the default branch if
  absent; never commit to the default branch directly).
- Follow the stages: fetch the current stage's prompt with
  `orchestra_get_prompt`; fetch the relevant playbook with
  `orchestra_get_skill` before producing a stage artifact.
- PRD before spec, spec before implementation. Run the project's tests
  whenever you change code; never claim green you didn't see.
- Commit at each meaningful unit with conventional messages. Write a devlog
  entry via `orchestra_devlog_entry` for the session and commit it.

## 3. Pause — gates as files

When you reach a decision only a human can make (stage approval, an
irreversible or taste-level choice, anything a playbook marks as a gate):

1. Write `.orchestra/work/<item>/gates/NNN-<slug>.md` (NNN = next number):

```markdown
---
status: pending
raised_on: {YYYY-MM-DD}
---

# Gate NNN: {one-line question}

## Question
{The decision, answerable in one line.}

## Options
1. {concrete option — recommended first, say why}
2. {concrete option}

## Read before deciding
- {artifact path}
- {artifact path}
```

2. Commit it, push the branch, and **end the session**. Do not proceed past
   an unanswered gate. Do not speculate about the answer. Do not start the
   next stage "provisionally."

## 4. Resume — answers are commits

A human answers by editing the gate file:

```markdown
---
status: answered
answer: "{their decision}"
answered_on: {YYYY-MM-DD}
---
```

When you find such a gate during orientation: apply the answer exactly,
mark the file `consumed: true`, and continue from precisely where the work
stopped. Never re-ask an answered question; never reopen a consumed gate.

## 5. Exit clean (every session, every path)

- Working tree committed, branch pushed. A session that exits with
  uncommitted work has failed even if the work is good.
- If you ran out of turns mid-task: commit what is consistent, note the
  exact stopping point in the devlog entry, and exit — the next session
  resumes from your note.","files":[]}

# relnotes Roadmap

**Objective:** A developer runs `relnotes v1.2.0..v1.3.0` and gets clean,
well-organized markdown release notes — changes grouped by type, noise commits
dropped, breaking changes highlighted — ready to paste into a changelog or
GitHub release.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Given any two reachable tags, the tool emits valid markdown release notes without manual editing
- [ ] Commits are grouped into meaningful categories (features, fixes, etc.)
- [ ] Noise commits (merges, chores, WIP) are dropped by default
- [ ] Breaking changes are detected and surfaced in a dedicated section
- [ ] The tool is installable and documented for end users

## Context

Maintainers and release managers today either hand-write release notes or paste
raw `git log` output. `relnotes` automates the curation: it reads the commit
history between two tags, classifies and groups the commits, removes noise, and
highlights anything that breaks compatibility — producing polished markdown in
one command.

## Milestones

| Material | Location | Status |
|----------|----------|--------|
| M1 — Walking skeleton: tag range → commits → flat markdown | .orchestra/work/m1-skeleton/prd.md | Done (merged to main, gate 001 approved, 33/33 tests green) |
| M2 — Curation: grouping, noise filtering, breaking-change highlighting | .orchestra/work/m2-curation/prd.md | Active — PRD drafted, gate 001 raised (strategy decisions + PRD approval) |
| M3 — Configuration & output options | .orchestra/work/m3-config/prd.md | Not Started |
| M4 — Distribution & documentation | .orchestra/work/m4-distribution/prd.md | Not Started |

## References

- ADR-000: [The Score](.orchestra/adr/ADR-000-the-score.md)

# Gate 001: Approve the M2 PRD and the four curation-strategy decisions

## Question

Approve the M2 PRD and confirm the four curation defaults (or amend any), so the
spec can be written? Each decision is taste-level and shapes the spec, so they
are gated together. A one-line answer accepting the recommendations is enough;
note any you want changed.

## Decisions (recommended option first)

**1. Classification strategy**
1. **Conventional Commits primary + keyword-heuristic fallback** (recommended) —
   industry standard, scope-aware, predictable; fallback keeps the tool useful
   on repos that don't strictly follow CC.
2. Pure heuristic (keyword matching only) — simpler, no CC assumptions, but
   fuzzier and less predictable.

**2. Default category taxonomy**
1. **Features (`feat`), Bug Fixes (`fix`), Performance (`perf`), Documentation
   (`docs`), Refactoring (`refactor`), Other (catch-all)**, with a **Breaking
   Changes** section floated to the top (recommended) — aligns with Conventional
   Commits / Keep a Changelog.
2. A smaller set (e.g. just Features / Fixes / Other) — less granular but
   simpler output.

**3. Default noise rules**
1. **Drop merge commits; drop types `chore`, `ci`, `build`, `test`, `style`;
   drop `WIP`/`fixup!`/`squash!` subjects** (recommended) — surfacing dropped
   commits via a flag is deferred to M3.
2. A narrower rule set (e.g. merges + WIP only) — keeps more commits visible.

**4. Breaking-change detection**
1. **Detect both the CC `!` marker (`feat!:`, `feat(api)!:`) and a
   `BREAKING CHANGE:` / `BREAKING-CHANGE:` footer** (recommended) — requires
   extending the git reader/model to capture the commit body (`%b`).
2. `!` marker only — no body capture needed, but misses footer-only breaks.

## Read before deciding

- .orchestra/work/m2-curation/prd.md  (the full PRD, with rationale per decision)
- .orchestra/roadmap.md  (objective and success criteria this milestone serves)
- .orchestra/adr/ADR-001-python-cli-shell-out-to-git.md  (M1 stack/decisions)

## How to answer

Edit this file's frontmatter to:

```yaml
---
status: answered
answer: "Approve PRD; accept recommended 1/1/1/1"   # or note amendments
answered_on: {YYYY-MM-DD}
---
```

The next session will record the resolved decisions (ADR / PRD update), mark
this gate `consumed: true`, set the PRD `status: approved`, and proceed to the
spec stage.

1	---
2	ticket: m2-curation
3	status: draft
4	created_on: 2026-06-11
5	---
6	
7	# M2 — Curation: Grouping, Noise Filtering, Breaking-Change Highlighting
8	
9	## Objective
10	
11	Turn M1's flat commit list into curated release notes: commits classified by
12	type and grouped under category headings, noise commits dropped by default, and
13	breaking changes surfaced in a dedicated section at the top. This milestone
14	delivers the product's headline value — the difference between `git log` and
15	release notes a maintainer can paste without editing.
16	
17	## Success Criteria
18	
19	- [ ] Commits are grouped under category headings (e.g. Features, Bug Fixes) in a stable, documented order, with one bullet per commit (subject + short SHA)
20	- [ ] Conventional-Commit subjects (`type(scope): subject`) are classified by their type; non-conforming subjects fall back to a keyword heuristic, and anything unmatched lands in a catch-all category
21	- [ ] Noise commits (merges and the configured noise types — e.g. `chore`, `ci`, `build`, `test`, `style` — plus `WIP`/`fixup!`/`squash!` subjects) are dropped by default and absent from the output
22	- [ ] Breaking changes (Conventional-Commit `!` marker and/or a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer) are detected and surfaced in a dedicated section ahead of the category groups
23	- [ ] Categories with no surviving commits are omitted (no empty headings); a range that is entirely noise still yields a valid markdown document that clearly states there are no notable changes
24	- [ ] All M1 behavior is preserved — range resolution, error handling, `--help`/`--version`, and stdout output — and the existing M1 test suite stays green
25	
26	## Context
27	
28	Part of the [relnotes Roadmap](../../roadmap.md). This is milestone 2 of 4 and
29	depends on M1 (merged, 33/33 tests green). M1 deliberately stopped at a flat
30	list to validate the riskiest plumbing — range resolution, git access, error
31	handling, markdown shape. M2 builds curation on that proven base.
32	
33	This is where the roadmap's objective is realized: "changes grouped by type,
34	noise commits dropped, breaking changes highlighted." Three of the five roadmap
35	success criteria are satisfied by this milestone (grouping, noise filtering,
36	breaking-change detection). The classification engine introduced here is also
37	the foundation M3 will make configurable.
38	
39	## Scope
40	
41	**In scope**
42	- Parse commit subjects as Conventional Commits (`type`, optional `scope`, `!`)
43	- Heuristic fallback classifier for non-conforming subjects (keyword matching)
44	- A default category taxonomy mapping commit types → output sections
45	- A default noise-filter rule set (merges, noise types, WIP/fixup/squash)
46	- Breaking-change detection via the `!` marker and `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer
47	- Extend the commit reader and model to capture the commit body (needed for footer detection) and the derived classification fields
48	- A grouped markdown renderer (breaking-changes section + category groups), replacing M1's flat renderer body
49	- Wire the classify → filter → group pipeline into the existing CLI flow
50	- Tests across all three tiers (unit, integration, e2e) and a README update
51	
52	**Out of scope (deferred)**
53	- Configuration files, custom taxonomies, custom noise rules, and flags to
54	  toggle filtering or reveal dropped commits (M3)
55	- Templating and alternative/custom output destinations (M3)
56	- Per-author or per-scope grouping, changelog merging, diff stats (not planned)
57	- Packaging and distribution (M4)
58	
59	The taxonomy and noise rules ship with sensible **defaults** in M2; making them
60	**configurable** is explicitly M3's job. M2 hard-codes the defaults resolved at
61	the gate below.
62	
63	## Materials
64	
65	| Deliverable | Location | Status |
66	|-------------|----------|--------|
67	| Commit classifier (Conventional-Commit parse + heuristic fallback) | `src/relnotes/classify.py` | Not Started |
68	| Category taxonomy (type → section, ordering) | `src/relnotes/classify.py` (or `taxonomy.py`) | Not Started |
69	| Noise-filter rule set | `src/relnotes/filter.py` | Not Started |
70	| Breaking-change detection | within classifier / model | Not Started |
71	| Extend `Commit` model (body + type/scope/breaking/category fields) | `src/relnotes/models.py` | Not Started |
72	| Extend git reader to capture body (`%b`) | `src/relnotes/git.py` | Not Started |
73	| Grouped markdown renderer | `src/relnotes/render.py` | Not Started |
74	| CLI wiring (classify → filter → group → render) | `src/relnotes/cli.py` | Not Started |
75	| Unit tests (classifier, filter, renderer) | `tests/unit/` | Not Started |
76	| Integration tests (fixture repo with mixed commits) | `tests/integration/` | Not Started |
77	| E2E tests (grouped CLI output, all-noise range) | `tests/e2e/` | Not Started |
78	| README update (curation behavior, defaults) | `README.md` | Not Started |
79	
80	## Open Decisions (raised at gate 001)
81	
82	These four decisions shape the spec and are taste-level / hard-to-reverse, so
83	they are gated for human approval. Recommendations below; see
84	`gates/001-m2-curation-strategy.md`.
85	
86	1. **Classification strategy** — *Recommended:* Conventional Commits as the
87	   primary signal, with a keyword-heuristic fallback for non-conforming
88	   subjects. CC is the de-facto standard (semantic-release, git-cliff,
89	   release-please) and gives predictable, scope-aware parsing; the fallback
90	   keeps the tool useful on repos that don't strictly follow CC.
91	2. **Default category taxonomy** — *Recommended:* a small standard set —
92	   **Features** (`feat`), **Bug Fixes** (`fix`), **Performance** (`perf`),
93	   **Documentation** (`docs`), **Refactoring** (`refactor`), and **Other**
94	   (catch-all) — with a **Breaking Changes** section floated above all groups.
95	3. **Default noise rules** — *Recommended:* drop merge commits; drop types
96	   `chore`, `ci`, `build`, `test`, `style`; drop subjects matching
97	   `WIP`/`fixup!`/`squash!`. (Surfacing these via a flag is deferred to M3.)
98	4. **Breaking-change detection** — *Recommended:* detect both the CC `!` marker
99	   (`feat!:`, `feat(api)!:`) **and** a `BREAKING CHANGE:` / `BREAKING-CHANGE:`
100	   footer in the commit body. This is why M2 must extend the reader/model to
101	   capture `%b`.
102	
103	## References
104	
105	- [relnotes Roadmap](../../roadmap.md)
106	- ADR-000: [The Score](../../adr/ADR-000-the-score.md)
107	- ADR-001: [Python CLI, shell out to git](../../adr/ADR-001-python-cli-shell-out-to-git.md)

---
ticket: m2-curation
status: draft
created_on: 2026-06-11
---

# M2 — Curation: Grouping, Noise Filtering, Breaking-Change Highlighting

## Objective

Turn M1's flat commit list into curated release notes: commits classified by
type and grouped under category headings, noise commits dropped by default, and
breaking changes surfaced in a dedicated section at the top. This milestone
delivers the product's headline value — the difference between `git log` and
release notes a maintainer can paste without editing.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Commits are grouped under category headings (e.g. Features, Bug Fixes) in a stable, documented order, with one bullet per commit (subject + short SHA)
- [ ] Conventional-Commit subjects (`type(scope): subject`) are classified by their type; non-conforming subjects fall back to a keyword heuristic, and anything unmatched lands in a catch-all category
- [ ] Noise commits (merges and the configured noise types — e.g. `chore`, `ci`, `build`, `test`, `style` — plus `WIP`/`fixup!`/`squash!` subjects) are dropped by default and absent from the output
- [ ] Breaking changes (Conventional-Commit `!` marker and/or a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer) are detected and surfaced in a dedicated section ahead of the category groups
- [ ] Categories with no surviving commits are omitted (no empty headings); a range that is entirely noise still yields a valid markdown document that clearly states there are no notable changes
- [ ] All M1 behavior is preserved — range resolution, error handling, `--help`/`--version`, and stdout output — and the existing M1 test suite stays green

## Context

Part of the [relnotes Roadmap](../../roadmap.md). This is milestone 2 of 4 and
depends on M1 (merged, 33/33 tests green). M1 deliberately stopped at a flat
list to validate the riskiest plumbing — range resolution, git access, error
handling, markdown shape. M2 builds curation on that proven base.

This is where the roadmap's objective is realized: "changes grouped by type,
noise commits dropped, breaking changes highlighted." Three of the five roadmap
success criteria are satisfied by this milestone (grouping, noise filtering,
breaking-change detection). The classification engine introduced here is also
the foundation M3 will make configurable.

## Scope

**In scope**
- Parse commit subjects as Conventional Commits (`type`, optional `scope`, `!`)
- Heuristic fallback classifier for non-conforming subjects (keyword matching)
- A default category taxonomy mapping commit types → output sections
- A default noise-filter rule set (merges, noise types, WIP/fixup/squash)
- Breaking-change detection via the `!` marker and `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer
- Extend the commit reader and model to capture the commit body (needed for footer detection) and the derived classification fields
- A grouped markdown renderer (breaking-changes section + category groups), replacing M1's flat renderer body
- Wire the classify → filter → group pipeline into the existing CLI flow
- Tests across all three tiers (unit, integration, e2e) and a README update

**Out of scope (deferred)**
- Configuration files, custom taxonomies, custom noise rules, and flags to
  toggle filtering or reveal dropped commits (M3)
- Templating and alternative/custom output destinations (M3)
- Per-author or per-scope grouping, changelog merging, diff stats (not planned)
- Packaging and distribution (M4)

The taxonomy and noise rules ship with sensible **defaults** in M2; making them
**configurable** is explicitly M3's job. M2 hard-codes the defaults resolved at
the gate below.

## Materials

| Deliverable | Location | Status |
|-------------|----------|--------|
| Commit classifier (Conventional-Commit parse + heuristic fallback) | `src/relnotes/classify.py` | Not Started |
| Category taxonomy (type → section, ordering) | `src/relnotes/classify.py` (or `taxonomy.py`) | Not Started |
| Noise-filter rule set | `src/relnotes/filter.py` | Not Started |
| Breaking-change detection | within classifier / model | Not Started |
| Extend `Commit` model (body + type/scope/breaking/category fields) | `src/relnotes/models.py` | Not Started |
| Extend git reader to capture body (`%b`) | `src/relnotes/git.py` | Not Started |
| Grouped markdown renderer | `src/relnotes/render.py` | Not Started |
| CLI wiring (classify → filter → group → render) | `src/relnotes/cli.py` | Not Started |
| Unit tests (classifier, filter, renderer) | `tests/unit/` | Not Started |
| Integration tests (fixture repo with mixed commits) | `tests/integration/` | Not Started |
| E2E tests (grouped CLI output, all-noise range) | `tests/e2e/` | Not Started |
| README update (curation behavior, defaults) | `README.md` | Not Started |

## Open Decisions (raised at gate 001)

These four decisions shape the spec and are taste-level / hard-to-reverse, so
they are gated for human approval. Recommendations below; see
`gates/001-m2-curation-strategy.md`.

1. **Classification strategy** — *Recommended:* Conventional Commits as the
   primary signal, with a keyword-heuristic fallback for non-conforming
   subjects. CC is the de-facto standard (semantic-release, git-cliff,
   release-please) and gives predictable, scope-aware parsing; the fallback
   keeps the tool useful on repos that don't strictly follow CC.
2. **Default category taxonomy** — *Recommended:* a small standard set —
   **Features** (`feat`), **Bug Fixes** (`fix`), **Performance** (`perf`),
   **Documentation** (`docs`), **Refactoring** (`refactor`), and **Other**
   (catch-all) — with a **Breaking Changes** section floated above all groups.
3. **Default noise rules** — *Recommended:* drop merge commits; drop types
   `chore`, `ci`, `build`, `test`, `style`; drop subjects matching
   `WIP`/`fixup!`/`squash!`. (Surfacing these via a flag is deferred to M3.)
4. **Breaking-change detection** — *Recommended:* detect both the CC `!` marker
   (`feat!:`, `feat(api)!:`) **and** a `BREAKING CHANGE:` / `BREAKING-CHANGE:`
   footer in the commit body. This is why M2 must extend the reader/model to
   capture `%b`.

## References

- [relnotes Roadmap](../../roadmap.md)
- ADR-000: [The Score](../../adr/ADR-000-the-score.md)
- ADR-001: [Python CLI, shell out to git](../../adr/ADR-001-python-cli-shell-out-to-git.md)
- [Conventional Commits 1.0.0](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/)
- [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/)

## Notes

The classifier is the architectural heart of M2 and of the product. Capturing
the commit body (`%b`) is a prerequisite for footer-based breaking-change
detection and is the one change that ripples back into M1's git layer — worth
calling out early. Once the four decisions above are answered at gate 001, the
resolved choices should be recorded (ADR or PRD update) and the work proceeds to
the spec stage.

{"name":"orchestra-spec","description":"Generate an execution spec from an approved PRD — define approach, steps, deliverables, acceptance criteria, and risks.","when_to_use":"Use when a PRD is approved and the work needs a concrete execution plan.","content":"# Write Spec

Generate an execution spec from an approved PRD. The spec is the contract between the conductor (you) and the orchestra (agents).

## Prerequisites

- A PRD must exist at `.orchestra/work/{id}-{name}/prd.md`
- The PRD should be approved (user has confirmed objective and success criteria)

## Steps

### 1. Read the PRD

- Find the PRD from $ARGUMENTS (path or work item name)
- Read the PRD completely — objective, success criteria, materials table, context
- Read the parent milestone PRD for broader context
- Read any referenced ADRs

### 2. Analyze the Codebase (if applicable)

If the work involves code changes:
- Glob for relevant files mentioned in the PRD
- Read existing patterns and conventions from CLAUDE.md
- Identify integration points and dependencies
- Note existing tests that may need updating

### 3. Design the Approach

Break the work into concrete steps:
- Each step should be independently executable
- Steps should be ordered by dependency
- Identify which steps could be parallelized
- Note which steps need human input vs. agent execution

### 4. Design the Testing Strategy

TDD is mandatory across three tiers. For every spec that involves code, design all three layers before writing the approach steps:

**Unit tests** — mock external boundaries (network, filesystem, DB). Fast, deterministic. Committed before the implementation they cover. Run as the project default suite — no external dependencies required.

**Integration tests** — exercise real subsystems at the boundary being tested (real API, real DB, real filesystem). No mocks at the boundary. Excluded from the default run — require external access. Must include any external API call so redirect behavior, auth, and response shape are verified against reality.

**E2E tests** — exercise the full user-facing interface from end to end (CLI invocation, HTTP endpoint, browser). Hits real external dependencies. Excluded from the default run — require the full running stack.

For each tier, specify:
- Which file(s) hold the tests
- Which behaviors are covered
- What is mocked (unit) or not mocked (integration/E2E)
- The commit ordering constraint (tests before implementation)

### 5. Generate the Spec

Generate the spec with this structure:

- **Title**: Matches the PRD title
- **PRD link**: Relative path to the PRD
- **Status**: Draft
- **Objective**: Restated from PRD
- **Approach**: Step-by-step execution plan with details
- **Testing Strategy**: Explicit unit / integration / E2E sections (see Step 4)
- **Deliverables table**: Concrete output files with paths and status
- **Acceptance criteria**: Separated into Functional, Unit, Integration, and E2E sections
- **Dependencies**: What must exist before each step
- **Risks**: What could go wrong and how to mitigate

### 6. Write the File

Save to: `.orchestra/work/{id}-{name}/spec.md` (alongside the PRD)

Include YAML frontmatter at the top of the file:

```yaml
---
ticket: {ticket-id or slug — matches the work item folder name}
status: draft
created_on: {today's date in YYYY-MM-DD format}
---
```

Status vocabulary for specs: `draft` → `approved` → `in-progress` → `complete`

### 7. Present for Approval

Show the user the spec. Ask:
- Does the approach make sense?
- Are any steps missing or out of order?
- Are the acceptance criteria specific enough for an agent to verify?
- Should any steps be broken into separate tickets?

## Quality Checks

- [ ] Every PRD success criterion maps to at least one acceptance criterion in the spec
- [ ] Steps are ordered by dependency — no step requires output from a later step
- [ ] Deliverables table has concrete file paths, not vague descriptions
- [ ] Risks have mitigations, not just "this could go wrong"
- [ ] An agent reading only this spec could execute the work without asking questions
- [ ] All three test tiers are present: unit, integration, and E2E — each with named files and covered behaviors
- [ ] Integration tests cover any external API or network call so real redirect/auth/shape issues are caught
- [ ] Acceptance criteria are split into Functional / Unit / Integration / E2E sections
- [ ] TDD commit ordering is stated explicitly for each tier

## Spec vs. PRD

| | PRD | Spec |
|---|---|---|
| **Answers** | What and why | How |
| **Audience** | Composer (strategic) | Orchestra (execution) |
| **Language** | Business/outcome | Tactical/technical |
| **Approval** | Before spec | Before implementation |
| **Lifespan** | Until milestone closes | Until work is done |

---

## TDD Policy

Every spec that involves code must plan for three test tiers. All three are non-negotiable. Each tier has a different purpose, a different mocking stance, and a different run profile.

### Tier 1 — Unit Tests

**Purpose:** Verify logic in isolation. Fast, deterministic, no I/O.

**Mocking stance:** Mock everything outside the unit under test — network, filesystem, DB, time. Use purpose-built mocking tools (e.g. `pytest-httpx`, `monkeypatch`, `unittest.mock`).

**Run profile:** Default — `uv run pytest` with no flags. Must pass on every commit.

**What they catch:** Logic errors, branching, edge cases, error paths.

**What they do NOT catch:** Redirect behavior, real API response shape, real DB schema changes, real filesystem permissions — anything that only exists at the real boundary.

**TDD constraint:** Test file committed before the implementation file it covers.

---

### Tier 2 — Integration Tests

**Purpose:** Verify that the code works correctly against the real external boundary — the actual API, the actual DB, the actual filesystem. This is where mocks lie and reality bites.

**Mocking stance:** No mocks at the boundary being tested. If you are testing an HTTP API integration, hit the real HTTP endpoint. If you are testing a DB layer, use a real DB. Internal helpers may still be mocked if they are not the subject of the test.

**Run profile:** `uv run pytest -m integration` — excluded from the default run because they are slow and require network/external access.

**What they catch:** Redirect chains, auth failures, unexpected response shapes, rate limits, SSL issues, schema drift — anything that only reveals itself against the real system.

**What they do NOT catch:** Full user-facing behavior or multi-layer interaction.

**TDD constraint:** Integration test file committed before the integration implementation it covers.

> **Mandatory rule:** Any spec that touches an external API, database, or remote service MUST include at least one integration test that hits the real endpoint. "We tested it with mocks" is not sufficient. Mocks encode assumptions; integration tests verify them.

---

### Tier 3 — E2E Tests

**Purpose:** Verify the complete user-facing interface from entry point to output, exercising the full stack with real external dependencies.

**Mocking stance:** Nothing mocked. The test invokes the system exactly as a user would — CLI command, HTTP request, UI action.

**Run profile:** `uv run pytest -m e2e` — excluded from the default run. May require live credentials or network access.

**What they catch:** Wiring failures between layers, misconfigured entry points, broken CLI argument parsing, missing env vars in production config, deployment regressions.

**What they do NOT catch:** Fine-grained logic (that's unit territory) or boundary-specific behavior (that's integration territory).

**TDD constraint:** E2E test file committed before the final wiring that makes it pass.

---

### Mocking Decision Table

| Scenario | Unit | Integration | E2E |
|----------|------|-------------|-----|
| External HTTP API | Mock with `pytest-httpx` | **Hit real endpoint** | **Hit real endpoint** |
| Database | Mock or in-memory | **Real DB** | **Real DB** |
| Filesystem | `tmp_path` fixture | **Real filesystem** | **Real filesystem** |
| Time / clock | `monkeypatch` | Real time | Real time |
| Internal functions | Mock if needed | Real | Real |

---

### Tooling by Stack

Pick the right tools for the stack. The three-tier rule is language-agnostic; the tools are not.

#### Python

| Tier | Tools |
|------|-------|
| Unit | `pytest`, `pytest-httpx` (HTTP mocking), `monkeypatch` (env/time/functions), `tmp_path` (filesystem) |
| Integration | `pytest` + `@pytest.mark.integration`, real `httpx`/`requests` calls, real DB via `pytest-postgresql` / `pytest-mongo` / SQLite |
| E2E | `pytest` + `@pytest.mark.e2e`, `subprocess` or `click.testing.CliRunner` against real dependencies |

Run profiles:
```bash
uv run pytest                        # unit only (default)
uv run pytest -m integration         # integration suite
uv run pytest -m e2e                 # E2E suite
uv run pytest -m "integration or e2e" # all live tests
```

#### TypeScript / Node

| Tier | Tools |
|------|-------|
| Unit | `vitest` or `jest`, `msw` (Mock Service Worker for HTTP), `vi.mock` / `jest.mock` |
| Integration | `vitest` / `jest` with real HTTP (`fetch`/`axios`), real DB via `testcontainers` or a local instance |
| E2E | **Playwright** — full browser automation, also works for API-level E2E via `request` context |

Run profiles:
```bash
npx vitest run                       # unit only
npx vitest run --project integration # integration suite
npx playwright test                  # E2E suite
```

#### Web UI (any stack)

For anything with a browser interface, **Playwright is the default E2E tool**. Prefer the **Playwright MCP** (`dev-playwright` skill) when generating tests — it drives a real browser, explores the UI, and produces assertions grounded in what the page actually renders rather than what the code assumes it renders.

```bash
npx playwright test                  # run all E2E specs
npx playwright test --ui             # interactive mode
```

Key Playwright practices for specs:
- Record tests against the real running UI, not against mocked API responses
- Use `page.getByRole` / `page.getByLabel` locators — more resilient than CSS selectors
- Test the happy path and at least one failure path (form validation, API error state)
- Run against a real backend (staging or local with seeded data) — not a mocked server

---

### The Rule in One Sentence

> Mocks are allowed in unit tests to keep them fast and isolated. Integration tests MUST hit the real system at the boundary being tested — no exceptions. E2E tests MUST invoke the real user interface against the real stack.

Violating this rule means tests can pass while the system is broken in production. A common failure mode: all unit tests pass against mocked responses, but the real external service returns a redirect or unexpected shape that the mock never encoded — crashing the system at runtime in a way no unit test could have caught.

---

### TDD as an Agentic Essential

In human-paced development, a regression is caught within minutes — someone runs the app and notices it's broken. In agentic development, an autonomous agent can execute dozens of steps, commit multiple files, and close a milestone before anyone looks at the output. Regressions don't surface until review, or worse, production.

**Tests are the agent's conscience.** They are the only mechanism that runs continuously, independent of the agent's intent, and reports objective truth about the system state. An agent that writes no tests is operating on faith that its own reasoning is correct — and agents, like humans, make mistakes.

**The "do no harm" principle:** Every spec must treat the existing test suite as a hard constraint. Before implementing anything new:

1. Run the full suite and record the baseline — every test must be passing
2. No new commit should leave the suite in a failing state
3. If a new feature requires changing existing behavior, update the affected tests explicitly and justify the change — do not silently delete or weaken assertions to make tests pass

**Regressions are silent failures.** A test that was passing and now fails is information. A test that was deleted to avoid a failure is deception. Agents must never remove or weaken a test to make a build green — if a test is failing, stop, surface it, and resolve the root cause.

**Milestones build on each other.** In a multi-milestone project like this one, each milestone's test suite is inherited by the next. The integration tests from Live Rates must still pass when Multi-Currency ships. The E2E tests from Basic Converter must still pass at Polish & Ship. The full suite across all milestones is the definition of "the system works."

> **For autonomous agents running long loops:** run `uv run pytest` (or the equivalent) at the start of every session before touching code, and at the end of every step before committing. A red suite is a stop signal, not a obstacle to route around.","files":["README.md","evals/evals.json"]}

**What they do NOT catch:** Redirect behavior, real API response shape, real DB schema changes, real filesystem permissions — anything that only exists at the real boundary.

**TDD constraint:** Test file committed before the implementation file it covers.

---

### Tier 2 — Integration Tests

**Purpose:** Verify that the code works correctly against the real external boundary — the actual API, the actual DB, the actual filesystem. This is where mocks lie and reality bites.

**Mocking stance:** No mocks at the boundary being tested. If you are testing an HTTP API integration, hit the real HTTP endpoint. If you are testing a DB layer, use a real DB. Internal helpers may still be mocked if they are not the subject of the test.

**Run profile:** `uv run pytest -m integration` — excluded from the default run because they are slow and require network/external access.

**What they catch:** Redirect chains, auth failures, unexpected response shapes, rate limits, SSL issues, schema drift — anything that only reveals itself against the real system.

**What they do NOT catch:** Full user-facing behavior or multi-layer interaction.

**TDD constraint:** Integration test file committed before the integration implementation it covers.

> **Mandatory rule:** Any spec that touches an external API, database, or remote service MUST include at least one integration test that hits the real endpoint. "We tested it with mocks" is not sufficient. Mocks encode assumptions; integration tests verify them.

---

### Tier 3 — E2E Tests

**Purpose:** Verify the complete user-facing interface from entry point to output, exercising the full stack with real external dependencies.

**Mocking stance:** Nothing mocked. The test invokes the system exactly as a user would — CLI command, HTTP request, UI action.

**Run profile:** `uv run pytest -m e2e` — excluded from the default run. May require live credentials or network access.

**What they catch:** Wiring failures between layers, misconfigured entry points, broken CLI argument parsing, missing env vars in production config, deployment regressions.

**What they do NOT catch:** Fine-grained logic (that's unit territory) or boundary-specific behavior (that's integration territory).

**TDD constraint:** E2E test file committed before the final wiring that makes it pass.

---

### Mocking Decision Table

| Scenario | Unit | Integration | E2E |
|----------|------|-------------|-----|
| External HTTP API | Mock with `pytest-httpx` | **Hit real endpoint** | **Hit real endpoint** |
| Database | Mock or in-memory | **Real DB** | **Real DB** |
| Filesystem | `tmp_path` fixture | **Real filesystem** | **Real filesystem** |
| Time / clock | `monkeypatch` | Real time | Real time |
| Internal functions | Mock if needed | Real | Real |

---

### Tooling by Stack

Pick the right tools for the stack. The three-tier rule is language-agnostic; the tools are not.

#### Python

| Tier | Tools |
|------|-------|
| Unit | `pytest`, `pytest-httpx` (HTTP mocking), `monkeypatch` (env/time/functions), `tmp_path` (filesystem) |
| Integration | `pytest` + `@pytest.mark.integration`, real `httpx`/`requests` calls, real DB via `pytest-postgresql` / `pytest-mongo` / SQLite |
| E2E | `pytest` + `@pytest.mark.e2e`, `subprocess` or `click.testing.CliRunner` against real dependencies |

Run profiles:
```bash
uv run pytest                        # unit only (default)
uv run pytest -m integration         # integration suite
uv run pytest -m e2e                 # E2E suite
uv run pytest -m "integration or e2e" # all live tests
```

#### TypeScript / Node

| Tier | Tools |
|------|-------|
| Unit | `vitest` or `jest`, `msw` (Mock Service Worker for HTTP), `vi.mock` / `jest.mock` |
| Integration | `vitest` / `jest` with real HTTP (`fetch`/`axios`), real DB via `testcontainers` or a local instance |
| E2E | **Playwright** — full browser automation, also works for API-level E2E via `request` context |

Run profiles:
```bash
npx vitest run                       # unit only
npx vitest run --project integration # integration suite
npx playwright test                  # E2E suite
```

#### Web UI (any stack)

For anything with a browser interface, **Playwright is the default E2E tool**. Prefer the **Playwright MCP** (`dev-playwright` skill) when generating tests — it drives a real browser, explores the UI, and produces assertions grounded in what the page actually renders rather than what the code assumes it renders.

```bash
npx playwright test                  # run all E2E specs
npx playwright test --ui             # interactive mode
```

Key Playwright practices for specs:
- Record tests against the real running UI, not against mocked API responses
- Use `page.getByRole` / `page.getByLabel` locators — more resilient than CSS selectors
- Test the happy path and at least one failure path (form validation, API error state)
- Run against a real backend (staging or local with seeded data) — not a mocked server

---

### The Rule in One Sentence

> Mocks are allowed in unit tests to keep them fast and isolated. Integration tests MUST hit the real system at the boundary being tested — no exceptions. E2E tests MUST invoke the real user interface against the real stack.

Violating this rule means tests can pass while the system is broken in production. A common failure mode: all unit tests pass against mocked responses, but the real external service returns a redirect or unexpected shape that the mock never encoded — crashing the system at runtime in a way no unit test could have caught.

---

### TDD as an Agentic Essential

In human-paced development, a regression is caught within minutes — someone runs the app and notices it's broken. In agentic development, an autonomous agent can execute dozens of steps, commit multiple files, and close a milestone before anyone looks at the output. Regressions don't surface until review, or worse, production.

**Tests are the agent's conscience.** They are the only mechanism that runs continuously, independent of the agent's intent, and reports objective truth about the system state. An agent that writes no tests is operating on faith that its own reasoning is correct — and agents, like humans, make mistakes.

**The "do no harm" principle:** Every spec must treat the existing test suite as a hard constraint. Before implementing anything new:

1. Run the full suite and record the baseline — every test must be passing
2. No new commit should leave the suite in a failing state
3. If a new feature requires changing existing behavior, update the affected tests explicitly and justify the change — do not silently delete or weaken assertions to make tests pass

**Regressions are silent failures.** A test that was passing and now fails is information. A test that was deleted to avoid a failure is deception. Agents must never remove or weaken a test to make a build green — if a test is failing, stop, surface it, and resolve the root cause.

**Milestones build on each other.** In a multi-milestone project like this one, each milestone's test suite is inherited by the next. The integration tests from Live Rates must still pass when Multi-Currency ships. The E2E tests from Basic Converter must still pass at Polish & Ship. The full suite across all milestones is the definition of "the system works."

> **For autonomous agents running long loops:** run `uv run pytest` (or the equivalent) at the start of every session before touching code, and at the end of every step before committing. A red suite is a stop signal, not a obstacle to route around.","files":["README.md","evals/evals.json"]}

"""The single boundary between relnotes and the ``git`` binary.

No other module shells out. Everything here goes through :func:`_run`, which
keeps the subprocess call in one place so unit tests can mock it and the
integration tier can exercise the real thing.

The commit log is requested in a delimiter-safe format: fields are joined with
the ASCII Unit Separator (``\\x1f``) and records terminated with the Record
Separator (``\\x1e``). Those control bytes cannot appear in commit text, so a
subject containing ``|``, ``..``, or other punctuation never breaks parsing.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import subprocess

from .errors import GitNotFoundError, NotARepoError, UnknownRefError
from .models import Commit

US = "\x1f"  # field separator (ASCII Unit Separator)
RS = "\x1e"  # record separator (ASCII Record Separator)

_FORMAT = US.join(["%H", "%h", "%s", "%an", "%aI"]) + RS


def _run(args: list[str]) -> subprocess.CompletedProcess:
    """Run ``git`` with the given arguments, capturing output as text.

    Raises:
        GitNotFoundError: if the ``git`` executable is not on PATH.
    """
    try:
        return subprocess.run(
            ["git", *args],
            capture_output=True,
            text=True,
        )
    except FileNotFoundError as exc:  # git not installed / not on PATH
        raise GitNotFoundError(
            "could not find 'git' on PATH; install git and try again"
        ) from exc


def assert_repo() -> None:
    """Verify the current directory is inside a git work tree.

    Raises:
        NotARepoError: if not inside a git repository.
    """
    result = _run(["rev-parse", "--git-dir"])
    if result.returncode != 0:
        raise NotARepoError(
            "not a git repository; run relnotes from inside a git repo"
        )


def verify_ref(ref: str) -> None:
    """Verify that ``ref`` resolves to a commit.

    Raises:
        UnknownRefError: if the ref does not exist.
    """
    result = _run(["rev-parse", "--verify", "--quiet", f"{ref}^{{commit}}"])
    if result.returncode != 0:
        raise UnknownRefError(f"unknown git ref: {ref!r}")


def read_commits(low: str, high: str) -> list[Commit]:
    """Return the commits in ``low..high``, newest first.

    Assumes both refs have already been verified with :func:`verify_ref`.

    Raises:
        UnknownRefError: if git rejects the range despite verification.
    """
    result = _run(["log", f"{low}..{high}", f"--pretty=format:{_FORMAT}"])
    if result.returncode != 0:
        raise UnknownRefError(
            f"git could not resolve range {low}..{high}: "
            f"{result.stderr.strip() or 'unknown error'}"
        )
    return _parse_log(result.stdout)


def _parse_log(output: str) -> list[Commit]:
    """Parse delimiter-framed ``git log`` output into :class:`Commit` objects."""
    commits: list[Commit] = []
    for record in output.split(RS):
        record = record.strip("\n")
        if not record:
            continue
        fields = record.split(US)
        if len(fields) != 5:
            continue
        sha, short_sha, subject, author, iso_date = fields
        commits.append(
            Commit(
                sha=sha,
                short_sha=short_sha,
                subject=subject,
                author=author,
                iso_date=iso_date,
            )
        )
    return commits

"""Render commits into a flat markdown document.

M1 produces a deliberately simple shape: a title with the range, a generated-on
date, and one bullet per commit. Grouping, noise filtering, and breaking-change
highlighting arrive in M2 and will replace the body of this renderer.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Optional, Sequence

from .models import Commit


def render(
    range_str: str,
    commits: Sequence[Commit],
    now: Optional[datetime] = None,
) -> str:
    """Return markdown release notes for ``commits`` over ``range_str``.

    Args:
        range_str: Human-readable range, e.g. ``"v1.2.0..v1.3.0"``.
        commits: Commits to list, in the order they should appear.
        now: Generation timestamp; defaults to the current UTC time. Injected
            for deterministic tests.
    """
    if now is None:
        now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)

    lines = [
        f"# Release notes for {range_str}",
        "",
        f"_Generated on {now.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}_",
        "",
    ]
    for commit in commits:
        lines.append(f"- {commit.subject} ({commit.short_sha})")
    lines.append("")
    return "\n".join(lines)

"""Command-line entry point for relnotes.

This is the only module that knows about ``argv``, stdout/stderr, and exit
codes. It wires the pipeline together:

    parse args -> parse_range -> assert_repo -> verify_ref x2
                -> read_commits -> render -> print

Every :class:`RelnotesError` is caught here and rendered as a single clean
``relnotes: error: ...`` line on stderr, returning the error's exit code. No
traceback ever reaches the user for an expected failure.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import sys
from typing import List, Optional

from . import __version__, git
from .errors import EmptyRangeError, RelnotesError
from .ranges import parse_range
from .render import render


def build_parser() -> argparse.ArgumentParser:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        prog="relnotes",
        description="Generate polished markdown release notes from a git tag range.",
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "range",
        metavar="RANGE",
        help="Tag range 'A..B', or a single ref 'A' (treated as 'A..HEAD').",
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--version",
        action="version",
        version=f"relnotes {__version__}",
    )
    return parser


def main(argv: Optional[List[str]] = None) -> int:
    """Run relnotes. Returns the process exit code."""
    argv = sys.argv[1:] if argv is None else argv
    parser = build_parser()

    try:
        args = parser.parse_args(argv)
    except SystemExit as exc:
        # argparse handles --help/--version (0) and bad usage (2) itself.
        return int(exc.code) if exc.code is not None else 0

    try:
        low, high = parse_range(args.range)
        git.assert_repo()
        git.verify_ref(low)
        git.verify_ref(high)
        commits = git.read_commits(low, high)
        if not commits:
            raise EmptyRangeError(
                f"no commits found in range {low}..{high}"
            )
        sys.stdout.write(render(f"{low}..{high}", commits))
        sys.stdout.write("\n")
        return 0
    except RelnotesError as exc:
        print(f"relnotes: error: {exc}", file=sys.stderr)
        return exc.exit_code


if __name__ == "__main__":  # pragma: no cover
    sys.exit(main())

5	
6	# ADR-001: Python CLI that shells out to the git binary
7	
8	## Context
9	
10	`relnotes` is a command-line tool that reads a git repository's commit history
11	between two tags and produces markdown release notes. Before writing the M1
12	spec we needed to settle three foundational choices that determine every
13	downstream file path and the distribution story: the implementation language,
14	how the tool accesses git, and the supported range syntax.
15	
16	Two stacks were weighed. **Go** produces a single static binary (trivial install
17	in M4) but is slower to write. **Python** is faster to author and ubiquitous on
18	developer machines, at the cost of a heavier install/runtime story. For git
19	access, the options were shelling out to the installed `git` binary (portable,
20	zero native dependencies, matches the mental model of maintainers) versus a
21	library binding (e.g. pygit2/GitPython — native deps, more surface area).
22	
23	## Decision
24	
25	Build `relnotes` in **Python**, accessing git by **shelling out to the `git`
26	binary** via `subprocess`. M1 supports two range forms: `A..B` and bare `A`
27	(treated as `A..HEAD`).
28	
29	Python was chosen for authoring speed and ubiquity; the heavier install story is
30	accepted and will be addressed in M4 (e.g. pipx / single-file build). Shelling
31	out to `git` avoids native dependencies, keeps the tool portable, and means the
32	tool resolves revisions exactly as the user's own `git` does. The narrow range
33	syntax keeps M1 focused; richer revision selection (`...`, multiple ranges,
34	arbitrary revisions) is deferred.
35	
36	## Consequences
37	
38	- The project is a Python package. Tooling is `uv` + `pytest`; tests follow the
39	  three-tier policy (unit with mocked subprocess, integration against a real
40	  fixture git repo, E2E invoking the CLI).
41	- `git` must be on the user's `PATH` at runtime. Its absence is a first-class
42	  error case the tool must handle clearly. This is a documented runtime
43	  dependency, not a bug.
44	- Git access is centralized behind one module (a `git` wrapper) so the
45	  subprocess boundary is mockable in unit tests and swappable later if a binding
46	  is ever preferred. No other module shells out directly.
47	- Range parsing is centralized: only `A..B` and bare `A` are accepted in M1;
48	  anything else is a clear usage error. M2+ may widen this.
49	- Distribution (M4) must solve Python packaging/install; that work is explicitly
50	  deferred and should not block M1–M3.
51

# ADR-001: Python CLI that shells out to the git binary

## Context

`relnotes` is a command-line tool that reads a git repository's commit history
between two tags and produces markdown release notes. Before writing the M1
spec we needed to settle three foundational choices that determine every
downstream file path and the distribution story: the implementation language,
how the tool accesses git, and the supported range syntax.

Two stacks were weighed. **Go** produces a single static binary (trivial install
in M4) but is slower to write. **Python** is faster to author and ubiquitous on
developer machines, at the cost of a heavier install/runtime story. For git
access, the options were shelling out to the installed `git` binary (portable,
zero native dependencies, matches the mental model of maintainers) versus a
library binding (e.g. pygit2/GitPython — native deps, more surface area).

## Decision

Build `relnotes` in **Python**, accessing git by **shelling out to the `git`
binary** via `subprocess`. M1 supports two range forms: `A..B` and bare `A`
(treated as `A..HEAD`).

Python was chosen for authoring speed and ubiquity; the heavier install story is
accepted and will be addressed in M4 (e.g. pipx / single-file build). Shelling
out to `git` avoids native dependencies, keeps the tool portable, and means the
tool resolves revisions exactly as the user's own `git` does. The narrow range
syntax keeps M1 focused; richer revision selection (`...`, multiple ranges,
arbitrary revisions) is deferred.

## Consequences

- The project is a Python package. Tooling is `uv` + `pytest`; tests follow the
  three-tier policy (unit with mocked subprocess, integration against a real
  fixture git repo, E2E invoking the CLI).
- `git` must be on the user's `PATH` at runtime. Its absence is a first-class
  error case the tool must handle clearly. This is a documented runtime
  dependency, not a bug.
- Git access is centralized behind one module (a `git` wrapper) so the
  subprocess boundary is mockable in unit tests and swappable later if a binding
  is ever preferred. No other module shells out directly.
- Range parsing is centralized: only `A..B` and bare `A` are accepted in M1;
  anything else is a clear usage error. M2+ may widen this.
- Distribution (M4) must solve Python packaging/install; that work is explicitly
  deferred and should not block M1–M3.

"""Unit tests for the CLI wiring with the git layer mocked."""

from relnotes import cli, git
from relnotes.errors import UnknownRefError
from relnotes.models import Commit


def _stub_git(monkeypatch, commits):
    monkeypatch.setattr(git, "assert_repo", lambda: None)
    monkeypatch.setattr(git, "verify_ref", lambda ref: None)
    monkeypatch.setattr(git, "read_commits", lambda low, high: commits)


def test_success_prints_markdown(monkeypatch, capsys):
    _stub_git(monkeypatch, [Commit("f", "abc123", "Add x", "A", "2024-01-01T00:00:00+00:00")])
    code = cli.main(["v1..v2"])
    out = capsys.readouterr().out
    assert code == 0
    assert "# Release notes for v1..v2" in out
    assert "- Add x (abc123)" in out


def test_empty_range_errors(monkeypatch, capsys):
    _stub_git(monkeypatch, [])
    code = cli.main(["v1..v2"])
    err = capsys.readouterr().err
    assert code == 1
    assert "no commits" in err.lower()


def test_unknown_ref_errors(monkeypatch, capsys):
    monkeypatch.setattr(git, "assert_repo", lambda: None)

    def bad(ref):
        raise UnknownRefError(f"unknown git ref: {ref!r}")

    monkeypatch.setattr(git, "verify_ref", bad)
    code = cli.main(["v9.9.9..v2"])
    err = capsys.readouterr().err
    assert code == 1
    assert "unknown" in err.lower()
    assert "Traceback" not in err


def test_malformed_range_exits_2(capsys):
    code = cli.main(["v1...v2"])
    err = capsys.readouterr().err
    assert code == 2
    assert "relnotes: error:" in err


def test_version_flag(capsys):
    code = cli.main(["--version"])
    out = capsys.readouterr().out
    assert code == 0
    assert "relnotes" in out


def test_help_flag(capsys):
    code = cli.main(["--help"])
    out = capsys.readouterr().out
    assert code == 0
    assert "RANGE" in out

"""Integration tests: the real git binary against a real fixture repo.

No mocks at the git boundary — this tier verifies the assumptions the unit
tests encode (log output shape, ref resolution, exit codes).
"""

import pytest

from relnotes import git
from relnotes.errors import UnknownRefError

pytestmark = pytest.mark.integration


def test_explicit_range_newest_first(fixture_repo, monkeypatch):
    fixture_repo.commit("Initial", tag="v1")
    fixture_repo.commit("Add feature")
    fixture_repo.commit("Fix bug", tag="v2")
    monkeypatch.chdir(fixture_repo.path)

    git.assert_repo()
    git.verify_ref("v1")
    git.verify_ref("v2")
    commits = git.read_commits("v1", "v2")

    assert [c.subject for c in commits] == ["Fix bug", "Add feature"]
    # Real short SHAs are non-empty and a prefix of the full SHA.
    for c in commits:
        assert c.short_sha and c.sha.startswith(c.short_sha)


def test_bare_ref_resolves_to_head(fixture_repo, monkeypatch):
    fixture_repo.commit("Initial", tag="v1")
    fixture_repo.commit("After one")
    fixture_repo.commit("After two")
    monkeypatch.chdir(fixture_repo.path)

    commits = git.read_commits("v1", "HEAD")
    assert [c.subject for c in commits] == ["After two", "After one"]


def test_unknown_ref_raises(fixture_repo, monkeypatch):
    fixture_repo.commit("Initial", tag="v1")
    monkeypatch.chdir(fixture_repo.path)
    with pytest.raises(UnknownRefError):
        git.verify_ref("v999")


def test_empty_range_returns_no_commits(fixture_repo, monkeypatch):
    fixture_repo.commit("Initial", tag="v1")
    monkeypatch.chdir(fixture_repo.path)
    assert git.read_commits("v1", "v1") == []

"""Shared test fixtures.

``fixture_repo`` builds a real, throwaway git repository in ``tmp_path`` for the
integration and e2e tiers — no mocks at the git boundary.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import subprocess
from pathlib import Path

import pytest


class FixtureRepo:
    """A small real git repo with a helper to add tagged commits."""

    def __init__(self, path: Path) -> None:
        self.path = path

    def _git(self, *args: str) -> None:
        subprocess.run(
            ["git", *args],
            cwd=self.path,
            check=True,
            capture_output=True,
            text=True,
        )

    def commit(self, message: str, tag: str | None = None) -> None:
        (self.path / "file.txt").write_text(message + "\n")
        self._git("add", ".")
        self._git("commit", "-q", "-m", message)
        if tag:
            self._git("tag", tag)


@pytest.fixture
def fixture_repo(tmp_path: Path) -> FixtureRepo:
    repo_path = tmp_path / "repo"
    repo_path.mkdir()
    repo = FixtureRepo(repo_path)
    repo._git("init", "-q")
    repo._git("config", "user.email", "test@example.com")
    repo._git("config", "user.name", "Test User")
    repo._git("config", "commit.gpgsign", "false")
    return repo

created_on: 2026-06-11

# ADR-002: M2 curation strategy — classification, taxonomy, noise, breaking changes

## Context

M1 shipped a walking skeleton: a tag range resolves to a flat, newest-first list
of commits rendered as markdown bullets. M2 turns that flat list into *curated*
release notes — the product's headline value. Four taste-level, hard-to-reverse
choices shape every downstream file and the M2 spec. They were raised together at
`m2-curation` gate 001 and approved by a human on 2026-06-11 ("approve — all four
recommendations accepted as stated"). This ADR records the resolved decisions so
the spec and implementation have a single durable reference.

The four decisions:

1. **How to classify a commit** into a category.
2. **Which categories** exist and in what order they render.
3. **Which commits count as noise** and are dropped by default.
4. **How breaking changes are detected** and surfaced.

## Decision

**1. Classification strategy — Conventional Commits primary, keyword-heuristic
fallback.** A commit subject is first parsed as a Conventional Commit
(`type(scope)!: subject`). If it conforms, its `type` drives classification. If
it does not conform, a keyword heuristic over the subject assigns a category.
Anything still unmatched lands in a catch-all **Other** category. CC is the
de-facto standard (semantic-release, git-cliff, release-please); the fallback
keeps the tool useful on repos that do not strictly follow CC.

**2. Default category taxonomy.** A fixed, ordered set of sections, each omitted
when it has no surviving commits:

| Order | Section | CC type | Heuristic keywords (fallback) |
|-------|---------|---------|-------------------------------|
| 0 | **Breaking Changes** | (any type, if breaking) | — (see decision 4) |
| 1 | **Features** | `feat` | add, introduce, implement, support |
| 2 | **Bug Fixes** | `fix` | fix, correct, resolve, patch, bug |
| 3 | **Performance** | `perf` | perf, optimi, faster, speed up |
| 4 | **Documentation** | `docs` | doc, readme, comment |
| 5 | **Refactoring** | `refactor` | refactor, rename, restructure, clean up |
| 6 | **Other** | catch-all | (everything classified but unmatched above) |

Breaking Changes floats above all category groups. The keyword lists are the
fallback only — they are consulted exclusively for non-conforming subjects, and
the resolved choices here are M2 defaults, not yet configurable (M3 owns
configurability).

**3. Default noise rules — dropped by default, absent from output.** A commit is
noise (and dropped) if any of:
- it is a **merge commit** (more than one parent), or
- its CC type is one of `chore`, `ci`, `build`, `test`, `style`, or
- its subject matches a `WIP` / `fixup!` / `squash!` marker (case-insensitive,
  leading-token / prefix match).

Surfacing dropped commits via a flag is deferred to M3. A range that is entirely
noise still yields a valid markdown document that states there are no notable
changes.

**4. Breaking-change detection — `!` marker and `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer.** A
commit is breaking if either:
- its CC header carries the `!` marker (`feat!:`, `feat(api)!:`), or
- its body contains a `BREAKING CHANGE:` or `BREAKING-CHANGE:` footer.

Detecting the footer requires the commit **body** (`%b`), which M1 does not
capture. M2 therefore extends the git reader and the `Commit` model to carry the
body. A breaking commit appears in the **Breaking Changes** section *in addition
to* keeping its normal type classification context (its bullet notes the break);
it is never dropped as noise even if its type would otherwise be filtered.

## Consequences

- A new classification layer (`classify.py`) becomes the architectural heart of
  M2 and the foundation M3 makes configurable. The taxonomy, keyword lists, and
  noise rules live as module-level defaults there.
- The git layer's `_FORMAT` gains `%b`, and `Commit` gains a `body` field plus
  derived classification fields (type, scope, breaking, category). This is the
  one change that ripples back into M1's proven git layer; it must preserve all
  existing M1 parsing behavior (the delimiter framing already tolerates
  multi-line bodies because records are RS-terminated).
- The renderer is rewritten from a flat list to grouped sections with a
  floated Breaking Changes block and empty-section omission. M1's title and
  generated-on header are preserved.
- All M1 behavior (range resolution, error handling, `--help`/`--version`,
  stdout) and the existing 33-test suite remain a hard constraint — green
  before and after.
- Because these are hard-coded M2 defaults, M3's configuration work has a clear,
  named surface to parameterize. No flags to toggle filtering ship in M2.

## References

- `.orchestra/work/m2-curation/prd.md`

# ADR-002: M2 curation strategy — classification, taxonomy, noise, breaking changes

## Context

M1 shipped a walking skeleton: a tag range resolves to a flat, newest-first list
of commits rendered as markdown bullets. M2 turns that flat list into *curated*
release notes — the product's headline value. Four taste-level, hard-to-reverse
choices shape every downstream file and the M2 spec. They were raised together at
`m2-curation` gate 001 and approved by a human on 2026-06-11 ("approve — all four
recommendations accepted as stated"). This ADR records the resolved decisions so
the spec and implementation have a single durable reference.

The four decisions:

1. **How to classify a commit** into a category.
2. **Which categories** exist and in what order they render.
3. **Which commits count as noise** and are dropped by default.
4. **How breaking changes are detected** and surfaced.

## Decision

**1. Classification strategy — Conventional Commits primary, keyword-heuristic
fallback.** A commit subject is first parsed as a Conventional Commit
(`type(scope)!: subject`). If it conforms, its `type` drives classification. If
it does not conform, a keyword heuristic over the subject assigns a category.
Anything still unmatched lands in a catch-all **Other** category. CC is the
de-facto standard (semantic-release, git-cliff, release-please); the fallback
keeps the tool useful on repos that do not strictly follow CC.

**2. Default category taxonomy.** A fixed, ordered set of sections, each omitted
when it has no surviving commits:

| Order | Section | CC type | Heuristic keywords (fallback) |
|-------|---------|---------|-------------------------------|
| 0 | **Breaking Changes** | (any type, if breaking) | — (see decision 4) |
| 1 | **Features** | `feat` | add, introduce, implement, support |
| 2 | **Bug Fixes** | `fix` | fix, correct, resolve, patch, bug |
| 3 | **Performance** | `perf` | perf, optimi, faster, speed up |
| 4 | **Documentation** | `docs` | doc, readme, comment |
| 5 | **Refactoring** | `refactor` | refactor, rename, restructure, clean up |
| 6 | **Other** | catch-all | (everything classified but unmatched above) |

Breaking Changes floats above all category groups. The keyword lists are the
fallback only — they are consulted exclusively for non-conforming subjects, and
the resolved choices here are M2 defaults, not yet configurable (M3 owns
configurability).

**3. Default noise rules — dropped by default, absent from output.** A commit is
noise (and dropped) if any of:
- it is a **merge commit** (more than one parent), or
- its CC type is one of `chore`, `ci`, `build`, `test`, `style`, or
- its subject matches a `WIP` / `fixup!` / `squash!` marker (case-insensitive,
  leading-token / prefix match).

Surfacing dropped commits via a flag is deferred to M3. A range that is entirely
noise still yields a valid markdown document that states there are no notable
changes.

**4. Breaking-change detection — `!` marker and `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer.** A
commit is breaking if either:
- its CC header carries the `!` marker (`feat!:`, `feat(api)!:`), or
- its body contains a `BREAKING CHANGE:` or `BREAKING-CHANGE:` footer.

Detecting the footer requires the commit **body** (`%b`), which M1 does not
capture. M2 therefore extends the git reader and the `Commit` model to carry the
body. A breaking commit appears in the **Breaking Changes** section *in addition
to* keeping its normal type classification context (its bullet notes the break);
it is never dropped as noise even if its type would otherwise be filtered.

## Consequences

- A new classification layer (`classify.py`) becomes the architectural heart of
  M2 and the foundation M3 makes configurable. The taxonomy, keyword lists, and
  noise rules live as module-level defaults there.
- The git layer's `_FORMAT` gains `%b`, and `Commit` gains a `body` field plus
  derived classification fields (type, scope, breaking, category). This is the
  one change that ripples back into M1's proven git layer; it must preserve all
  existing M1 parsing behavior (the delimiter framing already tolerates
  multi-line bodies because records are RS-terminated).
- The renderer is rewritten from a flat list to grouped sections with a
  floated Breaking Changes block and empty-section omission. M1's title and
  generated-on header are preserved.
- All M1 behavior (range resolution, error handling, `--help`/`--version`,
  stdout) and the existing 33-test suite remain a hard constraint — green
  before and after.
- Because these are hard-coded M2 defaults, M3's configuration work has a clear,
  named surface to parameterize. No flags to toggle filtering ship in M2.

## References

- `.orchestra/work/m2-curation/prd.md`
- `.orchestra/work/m2-curation/gates/001-m2-curation-strategy.md` (the approval)
- ADR-001: Python CLI, shell out to git
- [Conventional Commits 1.0.0](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/)
- [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/)

# Gate 001: Approve the M2 PRD and the four curation-strategy decisions

## Question

Approve the M2 PRD and confirm the four curation defaults (or amend any), so the
spec can be written? Each decision is taste-level and shapes the spec, so they
are gated together. A one-line answer accepting the recommendations is enough;
note any you want changed.

## Decisions (recommended option first)

**1. Classification strategy**
1. **Conventional Commits primary + keyword-heuristic fallback** (recommended) —
   industry standard, scope-aware, predictable; fallback keeps the tool useful
   on repos that don't strictly follow CC.
2. Pure heuristic (keyword matching only) — simpler, no CC assumptions, but
   fuzzier and less predictable.

**2. Default category taxonomy**
1. **Features (`feat`), Bug Fixes (`fix`), Performance (`perf`), Documentation
   (`docs`), Refactoring (`refactor`), Other (catch-all)**, with a **Breaking
   Changes** section floated to the top (recommended) — aligns with Conventional
   Commits / Keep a Changelog.
2. A smaller set (e.g. just Features / Fixes / Other) — less granular but
   simpler output.

**3. Default noise rules**
1. **Drop merge commits; drop types `chore`, `ci`, `build`, `test`, `style`;
   drop `WIP`/`fixup!`/`squash!` subjects** (recommended) — surfacing dropped
   commits via a flag is deferred to M3.
2. A narrower rule set (e.g. merges + WIP only) — keeps more commits visible.

**4. Breaking-change detection**
1. **Detect both the CC `!` marker (`feat!:`, `feat(api)!:`) and a
   `BREAKING CHANGE:` / `BREAKING-CHANGE:` footer** (recommended) — requires
   extending the git reader/model to capture the commit body (`%b`).
2. `!` marker only — no body capture needed, but misses footer-only breaks.

## Read before deciding

- .orchestra/work/m2-curation/prd.md  (the full PRD, with rationale per decision)
- .orchestra/roadmap.md  (objective and success criteria this milestone serves)
- .orchestra/adr/ADR-001-python-cli-shell-out-to-git.md  (M1 stack/decisions)

## How to answer

Edit this file's frontmatter to:

```yaml
---
status: answered
answer: "Approve PRD; accept recommended 1/1/1/1"   # or note amendments
answered_on: {YYYY-MM-DD}
---
```

The next session will record the resolved decisions (ADR / PRD update), mark
this gate `consumed: true`, set the PRD `status: approved`, and proceed to the
spec stage.

# M2 — Curation: Grouping, Noise Filtering, Breaking-Change Highlighting

## Objective

Turn M1's flat commit list into curated release notes: commits classified by
type and grouped under category headings, noise commits dropped by default, and
breaking changes surfaced in a dedicated section at the top. This milestone
delivers the product's headline value — the difference between `git log` and
release notes a maintainer can paste without editing.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Commits are grouped under category headings (e.g. Features, Bug Fixes) in a stable, documented order, with one bullet per commit (subject + short SHA)
- [ ] Conventional-Commit subjects (`type(scope): subject`) are classified by their type; non-conforming subjects fall back to a keyword heuristic, and anything unmatched lands in a catch-all category
- [ ] Noise commits (merges and the configured noise types — e.g. `chore`, `ci`, `build`, `test`, `style` — plus `WIP`/`fixup!`/`squash!` subjects) are dropped by default and absent from the output
- [ ] Breaking changes (Conventional-Commit `!` marker and/or a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer) are detected and surfaced in a dedicated section ahead of the category groups
- [ ] Categories with no surviving commits are omitted (no empty headings); a range that is entirely noise still yields a valid markdown document that clearly states there are no notable changes
- [ ] All M1 behavior is preserved — range resolution, error handling, `--help`/`--version`, and stdout output — and the existing M1 test suite stays green

## Context

Part of the [relnotes Roadmap](../../roadmap.md). This is milestone 2 of 4 and
depends on M1 (merged, 33/33 tests green). M1 deliberately stopped at a flat
list to validate the riskiest plumbing — range resolution, git access, error
handling, markdown shape. M2 builds curation on that proven base.

This is where the roadmap's objective is realized: "changes grouped by type,
noise commits dropped, breaking changes highlighted." Three of the five roadmap
success criteria are satisfied by this milestone (grouping, noise filtering,
breaking-change detection). The classification engine introduced here is also
the foundation M3 will make configurable.

## Scope

**In scope**
- Parse commit subjects as Conventional Commits (`type`, optional `scope`, `!`)
- Heuristic fallback classifier for non-conforming subjects (keyword matching)
- A default category taxonomy mapping commit types → output sections
- A default noise-filter rule set (merges, noise types, WIP/fixup/squash)
- Breaking-change detection via the `!` marker and `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer
- Extend the commit reader and model to capture the commit body (needed for footer detection) and the derived classification fields
- A grouped markdown renderer (breaking-changes section + category groups), replacing M1's flat renderer body
- Wire the classify → filter → group pipeline into the existing CLI flow
- Tests across all three tiers (unit, integration, e2e) and a README update

**Out of scope (deferred)**
- Configuration files, custom taxonomies, custom noise rules, and flags to
  toggle filtering or reveal dropped commits (M3)
- Templating and alternative/custom output destinations (M3)
- Per-author or per-scope grouping, changelog merging, diff stats (not planned)
- Packaging and distribution (M4)

The taxonomy and noise rules ship with sensible **defaults** in M2; making them
**configurable** is explicitly M3's job. M2 hard-codes the defaults resolved at
the gate below.

`gates/001-m2-curation-strategy.md`.
